/home/thedarthur/Documents/aoa-maqao/gnu-gcc/src/kernel.c: 61 - 106
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


61:     for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {


62:         unsigned mod = i % 4;


63: 


64:         float ci;


65:         if (mod == 0)      ci = sinf(b[i]);


66:         else if (mod == 1) ci = tanf(b[i]);


67:         else if (mod == 2) ci = 1.0f / cosf(b[i]);


68:         else               ci = 1.0f / tanf(b[i]);


69: 


70:         // Boucle j déroulée par 4 : les 4 cas sont connus à la compilation


71:         // car mod est constant sur toute la boucle j


72:         if (mod == 0) {


73:             for (j = 0; j + 3 < n; j += 4) {


74:                 a[i][j]   = ci / cosf(b[j]);


75:                 a[i][j+1] = ci / cosf(b[j+1]);


76:                 a[i][j+2] = ci / cosf(b[j+2]);


77:                 a[i][j+3] = ci / cosf(b[j+3]);


78:             }


79:             for (; j < n; j++) a[i][j] = ci / cosf(b[j]);


80:         }


81:         else if (mod == 1) {


82:             for (j = 0; j + 3 < n; j += 4) {


83:                 a[i][j]   = ci / tanf(b[j]);


84:                 a[i][j+1] = ci / tanf(b[j+1]);


85:                 a[i][j+2] = ci / tanf(b[j+2]);


86:                 a[i][j+3] = ci / tanf(b[j+3]);


87:             }


88:             for (; j < n; j++) a[i][j] = ci / tanf(b[j]);


89:         }


90:         else if (mod == 2) {


91:             for (j = 0; j + 3 < n; j += 4) {


92:                 a[i][j]   = sinf(b[j])   * ci;


93:                 a[i][j+1] = sinf(b[j+1]) * ci;


94:                 a[i][j+2] = sinf(b[j+2]) * ci;


95:                 a[i][j+3] = sinf(b[j+3]) * ci;


96:             }


97:             for (; j < n; j++) a[i][j] = sinf(b[j]) * ci;


98:         }


99:         else {


100:             for (j = 0; j + 3 < n; j += 4) {


101:                 a[i][j]   = tanf(b[j])   * ci;


102:                 a[i][j+1] = tanf(b[j+1]) * ci;


103:                 a[i][j+2] = tanf(b[j+2]) * ci;


104:                 a[i][j+3] = tanf(b[j+3]) * ci;


105:             }


106:             for (; j < n; j++) a[i][j] = tanf(b[j]) * ci;

Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option.

This loop has 824 execution paths.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = (x<0 ? 0 : x) (or MAX(0,x) after defining the corresponding macro)

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 326.50 to 159.00 cycles (2.05x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is not vectorized.
Only 15% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 326.50 to 36.00 cycles (9.07x speedup).

Details


6% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 1% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 8% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 14% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 4% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Performance is limited by:

  • reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck)
  • writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck)

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 326.50 to 239.25 cycles (1.36x speedup).


Workaround


  • Read less array elements
All SSE and/or AVX registers are used:
in that case, try to relax register pressure by reducing the unroll factor or splitting your loop
  • Write less array elements
  • Provide more information to your compiler:

    • hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop
    • use the 'restrict' C99 keyword



  


FMA


Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

Workaround


Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Too many paths. If you really need to analyze all of the 824 paths individually, rerun with max-paths=824
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths

Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option.

This loop has 824 execution paths.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = (x<0 ? 0 : x) (or MAX(0,x) after defining the corresponding macro)

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 326.50 to 159.00 cycles (2.05x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is not vectorized.
Only 15% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 326.50 to 36.00 cycles (9.07x speedup).

Details


6% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 1% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 8% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 14% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 4% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Performance is limited by:

  • reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck)
  • writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck)

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 326.50 to 239.25 cycles (1.36x speedup).


Workaround


  • Read less array elements
All SSE and/or AVX registers are used:
in that case, try to relax register pressure by reducing the unroll factor or splitting your loop
  • Write less array elements
  • Provide more information to your compiler:

    • hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop
    • use the 'restrict' C99 keyword



  


FMA


Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

Workaround


Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.
